[Thermo Fisher Scientific](https://www.thermofisher.com/ "Thermo Fisher Scientific")

# Extruding Biodegradable Packaging

The development of sustainable food products encompasses not only food but also biodegradable packaging. Biodegradable packaging can be made from a wide range of renewable resources, including plant-based materials like cornstarch, sugarcane, bamboo, dried citrus peels, and apple pomace. The use of biodegradable packaging aligns with the increasing consumer demand for eco-friendly products and sustainable practices. Many businesses are adopting biodegradable packaging as part of their commitment to corporate social responsibility and environmental stewardship – and to satisfy their customer’s needs.

[Extruders](https://www.thermofisher.com/us/en/home/industrial/manufacturing-processing/extrusion-compounding-equipment/instruments.html?icid=CAD_blog_food_2024July#twin-screw-extruders) are one of the technologies that play a crucial part in transforming materials, like fruit pectin, into a biodegradable packaging film.

## What is Biodegradable Packaging?

Biodegradable packaging refers to packaging materials that can naturally break down and decompose into the environment without causing harm or leaving behind any toxic residues. It is designed to minimize the negative impact on the environment compared to traditional packaging materials, such as plastic.

Biodegradable packaging is used for several reasons. First and foremost, it helps to reduce the accumulation of non-biodegradable waste in landfills and oceans, which can take hundreds of years to decompose. By using biodegradable materials, we can minimize the environmental impact and promote a more sustainable approach to packaging.

Additionally, biodegradable packaging offers the advantage of being compostable, meaning it can be broken down into nutrient-rich soil through composting processes. This can contribute to the circular economy by providing a valuable resource for agriculture and horticulture.

Biodegradable packaging can be made from various renewable sources, including plant starches, cellulose, and polylactic acid (PLA), which is derived from corn or sugarcane. These materials are chosen for their ability to degrade naturally through biological processes like composting or digestion by microorganisms.

The production process of biodegradable packaging involves converting these renewable raw materials into packaging materials through different methods, such as extrusion, molding, or film blowing. The specific manufacturing process may vary depending on the type of material used.

## Food Extrusion Includes Packaging Materials

[Food extrusion](https://www.thermofisher.com/us/en/home/industrial/manufacturing-processing/extrusion-compounding-equipment/applications.html?icid=CAD_blog_food_2024July#food-extrusion) is an established and highly versatile technique for the production of food, feed, nutritional additives and flavors, and biodegradable packaging. It enables a continuous and cost-effective means of production, and its precise control ensures a high and constant product quality.

Twin-screw extruders are extruders with two co-rotating intermeshing, self-wiping screws and are well-established tools for the mixing, compounding, and processing of materials.  (Read more about twin-screw applications in this application note:[*Relevant process parameters for twin-screw compounding*](https://assets.thermofisher.com/TFS-Assets/MSD/Application-Notes/LR70-relevant-process-parameters-twin-screw-compounding.pdf?icid=CAD_blog_food_2024July)*.)*

## How Biodegradable Films are Produced using Twin Screw Extrusion

The extrusion process begins by feeding the biodegradable raw materials, such as starch-based polymers or PLA (polylactic acid), into the [extruder](https://www.thermofisher.com/us/en/home/industrial/manufacturing-processing/extrusion-compounding-equipment/instruments.html?icid=CAD_blog_food_2024July#twin-screw-extruders). These materials are typically in the form of pellets or granules. The screws rotate within the barrel, conveying the material forward while subjecting it to shear, heat, and pressure.

As the material progresses through the extruder, it undergoes several stages. First, it enters the feeding zone, where the raw materials are compressed and gradually melted. Then, it moves into the melting zone, where the heat generated by the barrel and the mechanical energy from the screws further melt the material.

Next, the molten material enters the mixing zone, where additional additives, such as bio plasticizers, fillers, or colorants, can be introduced to enhance the film’s properties. The screws facilitate thorough mixing of the additives with the molten polymer.

After the mixing zone, the molten material enters the metering zone, where it is homogenized and conveyed towards the die. The die is responsible for shaping the molten polymer into the desired film thickness and width. Various die designs can be employed to achieve different film structures, such as flat films or films with specific patterns.

Finally, the extruded film is rapidly cooled using a cooling system, which can include air or water quenching. The cooled film is then pulled through a set of nip rollers, which control the film’s tension and speed, ensuring uniform thickness and smoothness. Optionally, the film can undergo additional processing steps, such as corona treatment or surface coating, to enhance its properties.

As an example of how twin screw extrusion is used in biodegradable packaging, this [short video](https://players.brightcove.net/3663210762001/default_default/index.html?videoId=6335993135112) introduces a small-scale extruder process setup transforming fruit pectin into a biodegradable packaging film. You can see the pectin powder being fed into the machine with a gravimetric twin-screw feeder, which then shows a peristaltic pump feeding water/glycerol mix, which moves the mix into a sheet die to produce the packaging film.

## Summary

[Extrusion](https://www.thermofisher.com/us/en/home/industrial/manufacturing-processing/extrusion-compounding-equipment/applications.html?icid=CAD_blog_food_2024July#food-extrusion) is a common aspect of food development and production while also serving a crucial supporting role in the creation of new functional foods. And in some cases, extrusion can play a part in producing packaging that is environmentally friendly.

Comparing Biodegradable vs. Traditional Plastic Films
=====================================================

1.   [Blog](https://rtgfilms.com/blog/)
2.   [Articles](https://rtgfilms.com/category/articles/)
3.    Comparing Biodegradable vs. Traditional Plastic Films

![Image 4: Comparing Biodegradable vs. Traditional Plastic Films ](https://rtgfilms.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Comparing-Biodegradable-vs.-Traditional-Plastic-Films-.png)

[![Image 5: rtgfilms](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/b17380e126607f00f91b4b95e354d538?s=40&d=mm&r=g)rtgfilms](https://rtgfilms.com/author/rtgfilms/)

[Articles](https://rtgfilms.com/category/articles/)

[October 10, 2024](https://rtgfilms.com/comparing-biodegradable-vs-traditional-plastic-films/)

When you go to the store, you don’t typically give much thought to the packaging film or lidding that helps your purchases stay in their packaging. Many items you buy daily are wrapped and secured by plastic packaging films.. According to the National Center for Biotechnology Information, the use of plastic packaging films has helped the food industry to reach new safety levels, but its continued use also has implications for the environment.

Traditional Plastic Films – Their Environmental Impact and Longevity
--------------------------------------------------------------------

According to the Alexandria Engineering Journal, as of 2021, the plastics industry was generating 300 million tons of plastic annually, much of which is used only once and then discarded. This discarded plastic could take hundreds of thousands of years to decompose. When the severity of this issue became apparent to environmental scientists, attempts were made to degrade plastic packaging trash by water, fire, methane, or carbon dioxide.

An Overview of Biodegradable Packaging Films
--------------------------------------------

The accumulating trash of non-degradable plastic has caused significant damage to water and the air in the areas where it is stored. Joint research from environmentalists and plastic film manufacturers resulted in the creation of several types of biodegradable packaging films. These new films can be composted or broken down. According to the National Center for Biotechnology Information, these biodegradable films have become a sustainable approach to non-degradable plastic films.

An Example of Biodegradable Films
---------------------------------

RTG Films, one of the world’s leading manufacturers of packaging films, makes biodegradable films from organic materials, such as plants, including sugar cane and corn starch. The husks and leaves of corn cannot be used for food but can be converted into packaging materials to wrap merchandise. These biodegradable films are transparent, have no traces of petroleum, and are odor-free. After being removed from the packaging where they were placed, these films can be broken down into carbon dioxide and water after 90 days.

Key Differences Between Biodegradable and Traditional Plastics
--------------------------------------------------------------

The key differences between these different types of plastic include their composition. While biodegradable films are made from organic materials, traditional films are made from fossils and crude oils. Biodegradable films produce a smaller carbon footprint. At the end of their usefulness, biodegradable products are made to easily be broken down by sunlight, oxygen, and water.

### How Biodegradable Films Break Down Compared to Traditional Plastics

When biodegradable films are made, enzymes are added to the surface of the films. When RTG Films makes their biodegradable films, they add PLA (polylactic acid), an enzyme that can break down the polymers in the packaging films. In contrast, although traditional plastic films will become brittle and oxidize after long periods of time, they cannot dissolve or be degraded.

### Applications for Biodegradable Plastic Films in Packaging

Many current applications of these biodegradable films are in the food industry. Emerging demands for biodegradable packaging films exist in the biomedical, pharmaceutical, and health sciences industries. Research on these packaging materials continues, and future applications are expected to be limitless.

RTG Films – A Globally Trusted Source of Packing Films
------------------------------------------------------

If your company needs packaging or lidding films, our team at RTG Films has been a trusted provider of those films since 1995. Our company makes master rolls of many varieties of packaging films in-house at its plant in Pennsylvania. Our factory is AIB (American Institute of Baking) certified for food safety. We remain consistently committed to providing safe, legal, and flexible packaging films for our present and future clients.

Our company remains committed to producing polyethylene films, which are consistently regarded as one of the world’s most dependable, affordable, and flexible plastic films. We will also continue to provide our acclaimed polypropylene films, which are in demand for their use in fill-and-seal operations. However, with a growing demand for sustainable packaging materials, RTG also offers [biodegradable plastic films.](https://www.rtgfilms.com/) These eco-friendly films have the same high-quality RTG’s loyal customer base has come to expect.

### Contact Us for All Your Packaging Solutions

RTG’s biodegradable films are made from feedstock—non-agricultural products that don’t deplete the global food supply and would normally be thrown away. Our biodegradable packaging films were made in response to industry demand for a packaging solution that will break down within 90 days of being composted. [Contact us](https://rtgfilms.com/contact/) today for more information about our choices of biodegradable plastic packaging and [lidding films](https://www.rtgfilms.com/lidding).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Biodegradable packaging films are materials that decompose naturally, breaking down into harmless substances without leaving toxic residues, thereby minimizing environmental harm.
Adopting biodegradable packaging is crucial in combating plastic pollution, as it significantly lowers the accumulation of non-degradable waste in landfills and oceans.
Traditional plastic packaging contributes to severe environmental issues, taking hundreds of years to decompose, leading to pollution of waterways and harm to wildlife.
